fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build out the session token auth service we've been discussing. Basically it should handle the full login/logout lifecycle for our API — when someone logs in with valid credentials they get back a token they can use on protected endpoints, and we need the usual stuff like being able to log out just the current session or nuke all sessions at once. The token should have an expiry (we talked about this in the standup, same approach as that credential refresh logic from the old auth module) and there should be a way to auto-extend sessions when someone is actively using the API. We also need to make sure stale or tampered tokens get rejected cleanly through the HTTP layer. Oh and one thing that's been a pain — users sometimes get stuck in a weird state when they have too many active sessions, so we need some kind of cap on that. The stored tokens need some lookup metadata that lines up with what was actually submitted. Also there's some config around token prefixes that needs validation before it blows up at runtime. Broadly speaking the HTTP responses need to be consistent and predictable across all these flows. Let me know if anything is unclear — I know I'm probably missing some details around the exact formats and limits but just check what we've done before.

trigger_keywords: token length
how long is the issued token
what is the character count of the generated token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The issued session token must be exactly 64 characters long.

trigger_keywords: default session lifetime
how long until a token expires
what is the default expiry duration
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default session lifetime is 10 hours. A token issued at 2020-01-01T00:00:00 will expire at 2020-01-01T10:00:00Z.

trigger_keywords: expiry format
how is expiry displayed
what format is the expiry timestamp returned in
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The expiry timestamp must be returned as a quoted ISO 8601 string with a Z suffix, e.g. "2020-01-01T10:00:00Z". When sessions have no expiry configured, the expiry field must still be present but its value must be null (unquoted).

trigger_keywords: logout route
which endpoint handles logout
what URL is used for single session vs all sessions logout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Single-session logout must use the route /api/logout/ and return HTTP 204 with a null body. All-sessions logout must use the route /api/logoutall/ and also return HTTP 204 with a null body.

trigger_keywords: login route
what URL handles login
which endpoint issues tokens
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Login must use the route /api/login/ and return HTTP 200 on success.

trigger_keywords: authorization header parsing
what happens with an empty auth header
how are malformed authorization headers handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An empty authorization header must produce auth_result=none (the authenticator does not attempt a token lookup). A header containing only the scheme with no credential produces error=missing_credentials. A header whose credential portion contains spaces produces error=credentials_contain_spaces.

trigger_keywords: default authorization scheme
what prefix does the auth header use
what keyword goes before the token in the Authorization header
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default authorization scheme keyword is 'Token' (e.g. Authorization: Token <value>). If a custom scheme is configured, the default scheme is no longer accepted and the custom scheme must be used instead.

trigger_keywords: session limit
maximum tokens per user
what happens when a user has too many active sessions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an account already holds the configured maximum number of active (non-expired) sessions, a new login attempt must be rejected with HTTP 403 and the error message 'Maximum amount of tokens allowed per user exceeded.' Expired tokens do not count toward the active-session limit.

trigger_keywords: auto extend sessions
does authentication refresh the expiry
when is the stored expiry updated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When auto_extend_sessions is true, every successful authenticated request updates the stored expiry to now + session_lifetime (default 10 hours). When auto_extend_sessions is false, successful authentication does not modify the stored expiry.

trigger_keywords: token lookup key length
stored key size
how many characters of the token are used as the lookup key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The persisted lookup key must be 15 characters long and must match the leading 15 characters of the submitted token.

trigger_keywords: token prefix validation
maximum prefix length
what error is returned when the configured prefix is too long
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The maximum allowed issued-token prefix length is 10 characters. A configured prefix longer than 10 characters must be rejected with error=token_prefix_too_long, and the response must include max_prefix_length=10 and the actual prefix_length.

trigger_keywords: invalid token HTTP response
what status code for expired token
what detail message is returned for a bad token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any invalid, expired, too-short, or tampered token presented to a protected endpoint must result in HTTP 401 with the detail message 'Invalid token.'. The route must be /api/ and the URL in the response must also be /api/.

trigger_keywords: protected resource body
what does the authenticated endpoint return
what body text comes back on a successful protected request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A successfully authenticated request to the protected resource at /api/ must return HTTP 200 and the body text 'User is authenticated.'.

trigger_keywords: stored token count after repeated logins
are multiple logins independent
does each login create a separate persisted token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each successful login call creates an independent persisted token. After N successful logins, token_count must equal N and stored_token_keys_present must be true.

trigger_keywords: expired token cleanup
are expired tokens removed from storage
what happens to the stored record after a token expires
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: After an expired token is used and rejected, its stored record must be removed (stored_expiry becomes null when queried). The cleanup happens as part of the validation path through the protected endpoint.

trigger_keywords: unauthenticated detail message
what message comes back when no credentials are provided
missing credentials 401 body
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no valid credentials are provided to a protected endpoint (e.g. wrong scheme), the HTTP 401 response detail must be 'Authentication credentials were not provided.'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same approach as that credential refresh logic from the old auth module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The auto_extend_sessions setting in feature6_session_expiry_refresh: when enabled, every successful authenticated request resets stored expiry to current_time + session_lifetime (10 hours by default); when disabled, the stored expiry is never updated after issuance.

context_pointers.vague_reference: just check what we've done before regarding the exact formats and limits
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Refers to the specific numeric constants and format contracts spread across the test cases: token length=64, lookup key length=15, default lifetime=10 hours, max prefix length=10, expiry format as quoted ISO 8601 with Z suffix, and the exact error/detail strings such as 'Invalid token.' and 'Maximum amount of tokens allowed per user exceeded.'

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented here — you'd need to check the existing implementation or test cases in the rcb_tests/public_test_cases directory for the exact contract.

feature1_login_issuance.json.description: Login issues session tokens and returns configured response fields.

cases.description: A valid credential exchange creates one persisted session token and returns a successful HTTP response with an expiry timestamp.
cases.expected_output:
route=/api/login/
login.status=200
login.url=/api/login/
login.expiry="2020-01-01T10:00:00Z"
login.token_present=true
login.token_prefix=
login.token_length=64
token_count=1
stored_token_keys_present=true

cases.description: Repeated valid credential exchanges create independent persisted session tokens.
cases.expected_output:
route=/api/login/
login.status=200
login.url=/api/login/
login.expiry="2020-01-01T10:00:00Z"
login.token_present=true
login.token_prefix=
login.token_length=64
token_count=5
stored_token_keys_present=true

feature2_logout_scope.json.description: Logout endpoints revoke the intended set of persisted session tokens.

cases.description: A single-session logout revokes only the presented session token and leaves other sessions for the same account intact.
cases.expected_output:
route=/api/logout/
logout.status=204
logout.url=/api/logout/
logout.body=null
before.total_tokens=2
after.total_tokens=1

cases.description: An all-sessions logout revokes every session token belonging to the authenticated account.
cases.expected_output:
route=/api/logoutall/
logout.status=204
logout.url=/api/logoutall/
logout.body=null
before.total_tokens=10
after.total_tokens=0

feature3_protected_resource_authenticati.description: Protected resource requests accept valid session tokens and configured authorization schemes.

cases.description: A request to a protected resource with a valid session token succeeds through the HTTP authentication layer.
When a custom authorization scheme is configured, the default scheme is rejected and the configured scheme is accepted for the same token.
cases.expected_output:
request1.status=401
request1.url=/api/
request1.detail=Authentication credentials were not provided.
request2.status=200
request2.url=/api/
request2.body=User is authenticated.

feature4_authorization_header_validation.description: Authorization headers are parsed consistently before token lookup.

cases.description: An empty authorization header is ignored by the token authenticator instead of producing a failed token lookup.
A header that contains only the authorization scheme is rejected as missing credentials.
A header whose credential portion contains spaces is rejected as malformed credentials.

feature5_invalid_and_expired_tokens.json.description: Invalid or expired tokens are rejected and expired token cleanup is observable.

cases.description: A request with an already expired token is rejected by the protected HTTP endpoint.
A request with a token shorter than the lookup key length is rejected by the protected HTTP endpoint.
A request with a modified token value that cannot match the stored digest is rejected by the protected HTTP endpoint.

feature6_session_expiry_refresh.json.description: Session expiry times are renewed only under the configured refresh rules.

cases.description: With automatic refresh enabled, a successful authenticated request extends the stored expiry and the token remains usable beyond its original expiry until the renewed expiry is passed.
cases.expected_output:
check1.status=200
check1.url=/api/
check1.body=User is authenticated.
check1.stored_expiry=2018-07-25T15:00:00
check2.status=200
check2.url=/api/
check2.body=User is authenticated.
check2.stored_expiry=2018-07-25T21:00:00
check3.status=401
check3.url=/api/
check3.detail=Invalid token.
check3.stored_expiry=null
original_expiry=2018-07-25T10:00:00

cases.description: With automatic refresh disabled, a successful authenticated request does not change the stored expiry timestamp.
cases.expected_output:
check1.status=200
check1.url=/api/
check1.body=User is authenticated.
check1.stored_expiry=2018-07-25T10:00:00
original_expiry=2018-07-25T10:00:00

feature7_session_limit_per_account.json.description: Login enforces a maximum number of active sessions per account.

cases.description: When an account already has the configured maximum number of active sessions, a new login is rejected with a forbidden HTTP response.
cases.expected_output:
login1.status=403
login1.url=/api/login/
login1.error=Maximum amount of tokens allowed per user exceeded.
valid_tokens_after=10
total_tokens_after=10

cases.description: Expired sessions do not count toward the active-session limit; after the last available slot is filled, the next login is rejected.
cases.expected_output:
login1.status=200
login1.url=/api/login/
login1.expiry="2020-01-01T10:00:00Z"
login1.token_present=true
login1.token_prefix=
login1.token_length=64
login2.status=403
login2.url=/api/login/
login2.error=Maximum amount of tokens allowed per user exceeded.
valid_tokens_after=10
total_tokens_after=11

feature8_token_storage_metadata.json.description: Stored token lookup metadata matches the submitted token prefix used for authentication.

cases.description: After authenticating with a valid token, the persisted lookup key has the configured key length and matches the leading characters of the submitted token.

feature9_token_prefix_validation.json.description: Issued-token prefix configuration is validated before use.

cases.description: A configured issued-token prefix longer than the allowed maximum is rejected with a normalized configuration error.
